figures.caption: Figure 3. Comparison of time-dependent C-index performance (mean and standard deviation) across 5 folds on 13 datasets. The proposed Survival In-context (SIC) method achieves comparable performance to the baseline models CoxPH, DeepHit, DeepSurv, XGBoost (XGB). SIC does not require hyperparameter tuning, whereas all baselines are tuned with 100 trials. * indicates p-value < 0.05 for the two-sided t-test of SIC compared to the baselines.
Table 1. Inverse cumulative hazard H−1 0 (y) for baseline survival distributions.

Figure 4. Dataset rank by method across 13 datasets. The proposed Survival In-context (SIC) method shows the highest mean and median rank compared to the baseline models CoxPH, DeepHit, DeepSurv, XGBoost (XGB).

Figure 1. Survival In-context (SIC) method contains two stages: (I) Synthetic Data Generation using Structural Causal Models (SCMs) for the generation of (A) covariates X and the related (η1, η2) label nodes, and (B) the proposed Survival Prior with survival quantile U and the survival function parametrization H−1 0 (∗, α, β) to generate time-event labels; (II) In-context learning on synthetic data with a specialized time-event embedding and survival head and loss. Created in https://BioRender.com.

Table 4. Time-dependent C-index (mean ± std across 5 folds). UNOS and SEER datasets have restricted access and exceed the pretraining prior (sample size) of SIC. ∗ indicates paired t-test significance vs. SIC (p-value < 0.05)

Figure 7. Time-dependent C-index of SIC framework with different Survival Heads: DeepHit (main option used in evaluation), DeepSurv (Katzman et al., 2018) and DeepEH (Zhong et al., 2021). The original implementation of the DeepEH method outputs NaN values for FLCHAIN dataset. This should not be considered for comparison.

Figure 5. Time-dependent C-index of SIC model across training stages: stage 1 trains on a prior with fixed sample size of 1024, stage 2 expands to 1K-40K, stage 3 to 40K-60K. Expanding beyond stage 1 improves performance on larger datasets, while stages 2 and 3 are nearly indistinguishable.
Figure 6. Time-dependent C-index of SIC model for 10, 50, and 100 bins discretization after stage 1. No single discretization consistently outperforms the others.
Table 3. Summary of hyperparameter search spaces

Figure 2. Visualization of a random batch of 512 generated datasets: (upper) time-dependent C-index estimated from CoxPH models fit on the synthetic datasets and (lower) Kaplan–Meier curves on the standardized time scale. The proposed synthetic data generation method demonstrates the ability to generate diverse survival curve patterns and outcome complexity.
Table 2. Dataset statistics. UNOS and SEER datasets have restricted access and exceed the pretraining prior (sample size) of SIC.
